title: Marker Chain
desc: The blue return markers and chain posts on the lower path need renewing before somebody comes back following the wrong certainty.

steps.text:
No one who works Fear Valley for long trusts memory without help. The lower path uses a simple code instead: red scrapes for descent, blue paint for return, and waist-high chain posts where the footing turns narrow or the wall begins looking persuasive in the wrong way. This morning the blue line has faded on the middle bend, and one chain span sags low enough to suggest confidence that the valley has not earned.

The path keeper shakes a tin of blue pigment and hands you a short brush. "Renew the return marks if you want the wall side," she says. "Or retension the chain spans if you prefer the posts. Either way, nobody should have to guess which way safety was supposed to be."

options.label: Renew the blue return marks along the wall
Retension the chain spans between the path posts

steps.text:
The blue marks sit at shoulder height where a shaken eye can catch them quickly but not mistake them for the red descent line lower down. You scrub the old chalky remnants away, paint fresh bands in the proper intervals, and step back after every few marks to be sure the sequence reads clearly around the bend and through the rim mist. Once restored, the return line stops looking optional and begins doing its real job: refusing debate.

The path keeper walks the bend without glancing at the floor once. That is her test. The blue bands carry her eyes cleanly toward the upper rail. "Better," she says. "In Fear Valley, getting back should never depend on optimism."

options.label: Seal the pigment tin and leave the wall speaking clearly

steps.text:
The chain spans are there for the moments when the path starts feeling narrower than it measured five minutes earlier, which happens here more often than anyone likes to discuss. One span has sagged from repeated use and another has slipped a link where the outer post took a hard jolt from falling stone. You tighten the loose line, reseat the slipped link, and bring the whole section back to the firm waist-high tension the keeper expects.

When finished, the chain answers the hand properly again. No lazy droop, no uncertain give, just a clear mechanical reminder that somebody built a way through this place and meant it to remain one. The keeper leans on the restored span and watches the rift below without expression. "Good. The valley may unsettle courage. It does not get the posts."

options.label: Hook the spare link away and leave the line secure
